institutional_history.description: Federation of Malaya Agreement defines 'Malay' for purposes of citizenship and the position of the Rulers. The institutional category 'Malay' is born here, before independence.

Federal Constitution Article 153 grants 'special position of the Malays and natives of any of the States of Sabah and Sarawak' covering quotas, scholarships, public service, business licences. This is the constitutional anchor of the cleavage.

New Economic Policy launches under DPM Razak. The administrative term 'Bumiputera' is operationalised, extending the constitutional 'Malays and natives' into a working economic category for ownership-restructuring purposes. 30% Bumi corporate-equity target set.
NEP succeeded by National Development Policy; the institutional Bumiputera category retained, the ethnic-restructuring target dropped from a fixed deadline.

Successor policies (NEM, Bumiputera Economic Transformation Roadmap, PuTERA35) renew the category with shifting metrics. World Bank 2025: 87% of Malaysian income inequality is intra-ethnic, not inter-ethnic — i.e., the category's binding cleavage function is increasingly under stress (T4A issue 1986).

primary_sources.title: Federal Constitution Article 153; Article 161A (definition of 'native' for Sabah/Sarawak)
World Bank Malaysia Economic Monitor 2025 — intra-ethnic inequality decomposition
Gomez, E.T. and Saravanamuttu, J. The New Economic Policy in Malaysia (NUS Press, 2013)

notes: Modelled per §9.2 as institutionally produced — the 'Bumiputera' category is a 1971 administrative construction layered onto the 1957 constitutional 'Malays and natives'. It is not biological and is not stable across time. Categories applicable to East Malaysia are partly distinct (over 50 native groups in Sabah and Sarawak with separately enumerated rights under Articles 161-161E).
